$comment: Glossary terms used in spelling (orthography.spelling.*) explanations not yet in data/glossary.json. Architecture reviews and merges; chats do not edit glossary.json directly. Checked against glossary.json v9. Kept minimal per house style (no inline expansion of glossary terms in item text).

doppie.short: Le doppie are Italian's double consonants (bello, cappello, anno). They are pronounced longer than single ones and are phonemic: capello (one p) is 'hair', cappello (two) is 'hat'.
doppie.long: Le doppie (the doubles) are geminate consonants — held about twice as long as a single one. Because the length is phonemic, a doubling can be the only thing separating two words: penna/pena (pen/pain), nonno/nono (grandfather/ninth), sette/sete (seven/thirst). Nearly every consonant can double except h and q (whose long sound is written cqu, as in acqua).

digraph.short: A digraph is two letters spelling one sound. Italian's are gl (/ly/ in famiglia), gn (/ny/ in bagno) and sc before e/i (/sh/ in pesce); gli is a trigraph.
digraph.long: A digraph is a fixed pair of letters standing for a single sound that neither letter makes alone. Italian's consonant digraphs are gl(i) = /ly/ (figlio, famiglia), gn = /ny/ (sogno, bagno) and sc + e/i = /sh/ (pesce, sciare). Because English has no matching spelling, learners tend to write them out (familia, banio) — the digraph is a unit and cannot be reduced.

elision.short: Elision drops a final vowel before a following vowel and marks the gap with an apostrophe: lo + amico = l'amico, una + amica = un'amica.
elision.long: Elision (elisione) removes the final vowel of a short word when the next word begins with a vowel, replacing it with an apostrophe: la + acqua = l'acqua, di + accordo = d'accordo, mezza + ora = mezz'ora. It is not the same as troncamento (truncation), which shortens a word without an apostrophe — masculine un (un amico) and buon (buon giorno) are truncated, not elided, which is why they carry no apostrophe.

troncamento.short: Troncamento is shortening a word without an apostrophe (un, buon, signor, qual). Unlike elision, nothing is 'dropped before a vowel', so no apostrophe is written — hence qual è, not qual'è.
troncamento.long: Troncamento (truncation) drops a final sound to smooth speech but, unlike elision, takes NO apostrophe because it happens regardless of what follows: un libro / un amico, buon giorno, il signor Rossi, qual è. The classic error is treating qual as if it were elided and writing qual'è; qual is simply the truncated form of quale.
